title: LC General B2 - Test 4 - Part 4
fullTranscript:
M: Welcome to Law Today. I'm Neil Richards. This week, I'm focusing on eyewitness testimony. To explore this subject, I have law professor, Diana Wilding joining me. Now, why should we not believe eyewitness accounts of crimes or accidents? After all, the eyewitness was there.
F: I'm afraid there are several reasons. Let's start with memory reconstruction... the key point is that a memory is not the same as, say, a video of an event. All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event, and it may not be reconstructed correctly, even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details. And it can be influenced by external factors. For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
M: I guess the key research here is Loftus and Palmer. In that study eyewitnesses gave different accounts according to the questions put to them...
F: Yes, their participants were shown a video of a minor car accident and then asked one of several versions of the same basic question, How fast were the cars going when they hit each other?
M: ... or when they smashed into each other! They found that replacing that one neutral, more objective verb 'hit' with a strong verb 'smashed' increased the witnesses' estimates of the speed the cars were traveling at by about 20%, didn't they? That could result in a very different judgement in court...
F: ...But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court. But it could be used, without any bad intention, by a police officer during an initial conversation for example, and then it becomes fixed in the memory of the witness.
M: OK, so that's an external factor. What about internal factors? I remember several studies which showed that, when presented with an image of a criminal holding a weapon, people were less likely to accurately remember height, age, ethnic group and so on because they focused on the weapon.
F: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations. If anything, the presence of weapons in actual crimes make witnesses more conscious of details like age and race.
M: While we're talking about age and race, is there any evidence that they influence the reliability of eyewitness accounts?
F: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky, but personally, I doubt it. Indeed, studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.
M: What about the age of the witness? Can't that be a factor?
F: Not on its own. For example, an elderly person with reduced eyesight, may still be very confident that their visual memory's accurate. I see it in court all the time. And people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy.
M: Surely, relying on how confident someone sounds is bound to lead to mistakes in legal cases. Although I can understand why witnesses who sound more confident seem more believable.
F: Yes, absolutely! Nonetheless, confidence alone doesn't guarantee the reliability of a witness's testimony.
M: Now, finally, I'd like to talk about what's known as 'inattentional blindness'.
F: Yes, actually at my university we ask all first-year students to watch a video made by two researchers who looked into this phenomenon, Simons and Chabris. It's of people playing basketball, and the viewer has to count the number of passes of the ball made by one of the teams. Then, at the end, we ask how many students saw the gorilla.
M: Sorry? The gorilla?
F: Yes! During the basketball game, someone in a gorilla suit comes and stands on the court. The first time I watched the video I completely missed the gorilla! On average, just under half don't notice it, even though it's so unusual. The point is... if you're paying very close attention to something, you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.

items.quote: And it can be influenced by external factors. For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
items.zh: 而且它可能受到外部因素的影响。例如，问题的措辞方式可以影响一个人的回忆。

units.text: And it can be influenced by external factors.
For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.

items.topicCue: 听到 eyewitness testimony/目击证词 的问题(录音里可能说成 memory reconstruction、external factors)→ 这题答案区到了,集中听女方举例说明。
items.signal: For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
items.cue: 注意女方在解释目击证词问题时，有没有举例说明外部因素如何影响记忆。
items.explain: 女方在提到“外部因素”后，紧接着用“For example”引出具体例子，说明问题的措辞如何影响回忆。听到“For example”这类短语时，要警觉后面会跟具体的例子或细节，这往往是理解抽象概念的关键。
items._question: What problem with eyewitness evidence does Diana point out?
_options: the effect of particular words used to trigger recall
the tendency of witnesses to add details whenever they recall an event
the fact that witnesses sometimes want to be the centre of attention

items.quote: But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court.
items.zh: 但是，在法庭上不能问使用‘smashed’这样的问题。

units.text: But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court.

items.topicCue: 听到 subjective language/主观措辞 (录音里可能说成 strong verb 'smashed')→ 这题答案区到了,集中听女方对这种措辞的看法。
items.cue: 注意女方对“smashed”这种主观用词在法庭上的使用有什么看法。
items.explain: 女方直接指出“But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court”，明确表示这种主观措辞在法庭上是不允许的。听到这种直接的否定判断（couldn't be asked），要警觉这通常是关于规则、限制或明确结论的关键信息。
items._question: According to Diana, using more subjective language when questioning eyewitnesses
_options: only makes a small difference to their evidence.
would not be allowed in court.
might be done deliberately by police officers.

items.quote: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations.
items.zh: 但是，实际上，在现实世界中情况似乎并非如此。

units.text: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations.

items.topicCue: 听到 weapons/武器 (录音里可能说成 criminal holding a weapon)→ 这题答案区到了,集中听女方对男方观点的回应。
items.signal: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case
items.cue: 注意女方对男方关于“武器会减少细节回忆”的观点是同意还是反对，以及她反对的理由。
items.explain: 女方用“But, actually”明确表示了转折和反对，并指出“that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations”，暗示男方提到的研究结果可能只适用于实验条件而非真实世界。听到“But, actually”这类转折词时，要警觉说话者即将提出不同意见或修正之前的观点。
items._question: Diana objects to Neil's point about weapons reducing the recall of detail because
_options: not enough studies have been done to prove the point.
the studies which showed an effect were poorly conducted.
the effect was only observed under experimental conditions.

items.quote: but personally, I doubt it. Indeed, studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.
items.zh: 但我个人对此表示怀疑。事实上，研究表明，生活在多元化社区可以显著提高一个人识别和记住来自不同种族背景个体信息的能力。

units.text: Indeed, studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.

items.topicCue: 听到 race/种族 (录音里可能说成 racial backgrounds、diverse community)→ 这题答案区到了,集中听女方对种族影响目击证词可靠性的看法。
items.signalType: 反对/不同意, 结论/重点
items.signal: but personally, I doubt it. Indeed, studies indicate that...
items.cue: 注意女方对“种族是否影响目击证词可靠性”的观点，以及她是否有证据支持。
items.explain: 女方先用“but personally, I doubt it”表达了她对“跨种族识别困难”这一观点的怀疑，然后用“Indeed, studies indicate that”引出支持她怀疑的证据，即多元社区生活能增强识别能力。听到“I doubt it”这类表达怀疑的词语，以及“Indeed”这类表示强调和引入证据的词语时，要警觉说话者正在提出自己的明确立场并提供支持。
items._question: What does Diana say about race and eyewitness evidence?
_options: There's little evidence that one race is better than another at recalling events
Identifying someone from a different race is affected by the witness's prejudices.
In a multicultural society, race isn't a barrier to accurate identification.

items.quote: And people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy. Yes, absolutely!
items.zh: 陪审团成员非常看重自信，他们将其等同于准确性。是的，完全正确！

units.text: And people on juries place great weight on confidence,

items.topicCue: 听到 confidence/自信 和 accuracy/准确性 (录音里可能说成 great weight on confidence、equate it with accuracy)→ 这题答案区到了,集中听双方对自信与准确性关系的共识。
items.signal: place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy. Yes, absolutely!
items.cue: 注意男方提出一个关于陪审团如何看待自信的观点后，女方是否表示同意。
items.explain: 男方指出“people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy”，女方立刻用“Yes, absolutely!”表示了完全的同意和共识。听到“Yes, absolutely!”这类明确表示赞同的短语时，要警觉这通常是双方达成共识或确认某个事实的关键点。
items._question: What do the speakers conclude about the relationship between confidence and accuracy?
_options: Confident older witnesses may provide more accurate statements
A person confident in their memory is usually believed in court.
Confidence comes from having a good memory in general.

items.quote: The point is... if you're paying very close attention to something, you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.
items.zh: 关键是……如果你非常专注地关注某件事，你可能会错过其他进入视野的事物，无论它们多么不寻常。

units.text: if you're paying very close attention to something,
you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.

items.topicCue: 听到 gorilla story/大猩猩故事 (录音里可能说成 inattentional blindness)→ 这题答案区到了,集中听女方总结这个故事的“point”。
items.cue: 注意女方在讲完大猩猩故事后，有没有总结这个故事的“重点”或“寓意”。
items.explain: 女方在讲述完大猩猩实验后，用“The point is...”明确引出了这个故事的结论和寓意，即“如果你非常专注地关注某件事，你可能会错过其他进入视野的事物”。听到“The point is”、“The implication is”这类短语时，要警觉说话者正在总结或揭示核心信息，这往往是理解主旨的关键。
items._question: The implication of the gorilla story is that
_options: a person who witnesses a crime may miss key details.
eyewitnesses focus on the most unusual part of an event.
you can only believe about 50% of what people say.
